cpr_adult.title: CPR — Adult
cpr_adult.subtitle: For adults and teenagers 13 and older

steps.instruction: Check that the scene is safe before approaching.
steps.detail: Look for traffic, fire, electrical hazards, unstable structures, or dangerous people. Do not put yourself at risk.

steps.instruction: Tap the person's shoulders firmly and shout: 'Are you okay?'
steps.detail: If they respond, keep them still and call for help. If there is no response, they are unresponsive — proceed immediately.

steps.instruction: Call 911 now, or send someone else to call while you begin.
steps.detail: Tell the dispatcher your location, that the person is unresponsive and not breathing normally. Stay on the line — the dispatcher will guide you.
steps.warning: Do not leave the person alone to call 911 if you are the only one present. Call first if a phone is within reach, then start CPR.

steps.instruction: Roll the person onto their back on a firm, flat surface.
steps.detail: A hard floor gives your compressions the most effect. A soft mattress absorbs force and reduces effectiveness significantly.

steps.instruction: Place the heel of one hand on the center of the chest, between the nipples. Place your other hand on top and interlace your fingers.
steps.detail: Keep your fingers lifted off the chest — only the heel of your bottom hand should touch. Lock your elbows straight.
steps.warning: Do not place your hands too low — you will press on the stomach rather than the heart.

steps.instruction: Push hard and fast — compress the chest at least 2 inches deep at a rate of 100 to 120 times per minute.
steps.detail: Let the chest fully rise between each compression. Count out loud to keep your rhythm. The metronome below will guide your pace.
steps.warning: It is normal to feel or hear ribs crack during compressions. Do not stop — effective compressions are more important.

steps.instruction: After 30 compressions, open the airway: tilt the head back gently and lift the chin.
steps.detail: Place one hand on the forehead and two fingers under the chin. Tilt until the mouth slightly opens and the airway is straight.

steps.instruction: Give 2 rescue breaths: pinch the nose shut, seal your mouth over theirs, and breathe in for one second each.
steps.detail: Watch for the chest to rise with each breath. If it does not rise, reposition the head and try once more before resuming compressions.
steps.warning: If you are not trained in rescue breaths or are uncomfortable, skip this step and continue with compressions only — compression-only CPR still saves lives.

steps.instruction: Continue the cycle: 30 compressions then 2 breaths. Do not stop.
steps.detail: Continue until the person shows signs of life, an AED becomes available, a trained responder takes over, or you are physically unable to continue.
steps.warning: If an AED is available nearby, use it as soon as possible. AED instructions are simple and the device will guide you.

cpr_infant.title: CPR — Infant / Child
cpr_infant.subtitle: For infants under 1 year and children under 12

steps.instruction: Check for responsiveness: flick the bottom of the infant's foot. For a child, tap the shoulders and call their name.
steps.detail: Any movement, cry, or sound means they are responsive. No response means proceed immediately.

steps.instruction: Call 911 immediately or have someone call while you begin.
steps.detail: If you are alone with an infant, perform 2 minutes of CPR first, then call 911 — infants often respond within that time.

steps.instruction: Place the infant on a firm flat surface on their back. For a child, position as you would an adult.
For an infant: place two fingers on the center of the chest, just below the nipple line. For a child: use one or two hands as needed for their size.
steps.detail: Infant compressions use only two fingers — never a full hand. Child compressions use the heel of one hand on the center of the chest.
steps.warning: Two fingers only for infants — full hand compressions can cause serious internal injuries.

steps.instruction: Compress 1.5 inches deep for infants, 2 inches for children. Push at 100 to 120 compressions per minute.
steps.detail: Allow the chest to fully recoil between each compression. The metronome below will guide your rhythm.
steps.warning: It is normal to feel movement under your fingers during compressions. Do not stop.

steps.instruction: After 30 compressions, open the airway: for an infant, tilt the head to a neutral position — just slightly back, not all the way.
steps.detail: An infant's neck is very flexible. Over-tilting can close the airway. Just move to a neutral position so the nose points straight up.
steps.warning: Do not tilt an infant's head as far back as an adult's — this can block rather than open the airway.

steps.instruction: For an infant: cover both the nose and mouth with your mouth and give 2 small gentle breaths. For a child: pinch the nose and cover the mouth as with an adult.
steps.detail: Each breath should be just enough to make the chest rise — about 1 second each. Too much air can cause stomach inflation.

steps.instruction: Continue 30 compressions then 2 breaths. Do not stop until help arrives or the infant or child shows signs of life.
steps.warning: If an AED is available and the child is over 1 year old, use it. Use pediatric pads if available.

steps.instruction: Call 911 immediately.
steps.detail: Life-threatening bleeding requires emergency medical response. Make the call before doing anything else if another person can help.

steps.instruction: Protect yourself: put on gloves if available, or use a plastic bag, extra clothing, or any barrier between your hands and the blood.
steps.detail: This protects you from bloodborne infection. Bare hands are acceptable if no barrier is available — your priority is stopping the bleeding.

steps.instruction: Apply firm direct pressure to the wound using the cleanest cloth available — gauze, a shirt, a towel.
steps.detail: Press with the heel of your hand using your full body weight. The pressure must be firm and uninterrupted.

steps.instruction: Hold firm continuous pressure for at least 10 minutes without lifting the cloth to check.
steps.detail: Checking the wound disrupts the clotting process and restarts the bleeding clock. The timer below will track your 10 minutes.
steps.warning: Do not lift the cloth to check. If blood soaks through, add more cloth on top and press harder — never remove the first layer.

steps.instruction: If the bleeding is from a limb and cannot be controlled with pressure, apply a tourniquet 2 to 3 inches above the wound.
steps.detail: Twist the tourniquet until the bleeding stops. Note the exact time of application on the person's skin with a pen if possible.
steps.warning: Once applied, do not remove the tourniquet under any circumstances. Only paramedics should remove it.

steps.instruction: Elevate the injured area above the level of the heart if possible without causing more pain or injury.
steps.detail: Gravity reduces blood flow to the area and supports clotting. Do not elevate if you suspect a bone fracture.

steps.instruction: Keep the person warm and as calm as possible. Treat for shock: lay them flat and elevate their legs about 12 inches unless a head, neck, or back injury is suspected.
steps.detail: Severe blood loss causes shock. The person may feel cold, confused, and very thirsty. Reassure them help is coming.
steps.warning: Do not give food or water — surgery may be required on arrival.

steps.instruction: Call 911 immediately. Anaphylaxis can become fatal within minutes.
steps.detail: Even if an EpiPen is available, 911 must still be called. Epinephrine provides temporary relief — the person needs emergency evaluation.

steps.instruction: Check for an epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen). Check the person's bag, pockets, and any medical alert bracelet or wallet card.
steps.detail: Many people with known severe allergies carry an EpiPen. A bystander may also have one. Ask loudly: 'Does anyone have an EpiPen?'

steps.instruction: If an EpiPen is available: remove the blue safety cap. Press the orange tip firmly against the outer mid-thigh — through clothing is acceptable. Hold for 10 seconds.
steps.detail: You will hear a click when it activates. Keep firm pressure for the full 10 seconds to ensure the full dose is delivered.
steps.warning: Inject into the outer thigh only — never into a vein, buttock, or hand. If accidentally injected into a hand or finger, tell paramedics immediately.

steps.instruction: After injection, massage the injection site for 10 seconds and note the time.
steps.detail: Epinephrine begins working within minutes. Symptoms may improve quickly — do not let the person get up or walk.

steps.instruction: Position the person: lay them flat on their back with legs elevated. If they are having difficulty breathing, let them sit up slightly.
steps.detail: If unconscious, place them on their side in the recovery position to prevent choking. Never let someone in anaphylaxis stand or walk.
steps.warning: If the person becomes unconscious and stops breathing normally, begin CPR immediately.

steps.instruction: If symptoms do not improve or return within 5 to 15 minutes, use a second EpiPen if available.
steps.detail: Many EpiPen kits come with two doses. Biphasic reactions — where symptoms return after initial improvement — are common in anaphylaxis.
steps.warning: Antihistamines like Benadryl are not effective for anaphylaxis. Do not substitute antihistamines for epinephrine.

steps.instruction: Stay with the person until paramedics arrive. Keep them calm, warm, and still.
steps.detail: Even if they feel completely better, hospital evaluation is essential. Anaphylaxis can return hours later without a second exposure.

steps.instruction: Call 911 immediately.
steps.detail: Time is critical in a heart attack. Every minute without treatment causes more heart muscle damage. Do not wait to see if symptoms improve.

steps.instruction: Have the person sit or lie in the most comfortable position — often sitting up slightly, leaning back. Do not let them walk.
steps.detail: Physical exertion places more demand on an already stressed heart. Help them to the floor or a chair and keep them there.

steps.instruction: Loosen any tight clothing around the neck and chest: collars, ties, scarves, bra straps.
steps.detail: Restricted clothing can increase discomfort and impede breathing.

steps.instruction: If the person is conscious, not allergic to aspirin, and has no known reason to avoid it: have them chew one regular adult aspirin (325 mg) or four low-dose aspirin (81 mg each).
steps.detail: Chewing rather than swallowing gets aspirin into the bloodstream faster. Aspirin reduces blood clot formation and can limit heart damage.
steps.warning: Do not give aspirin if the person is allergic, is already taking blood thinners, has active bleeding, or has had recent surgery. When in doubt, skip it.

steps.instruction: Stay with the person, keep them calm, and speak reassuringly. Panic and anxiety increase the heart's oxygen demand.
steps.detail: Tell them help is coming, that they are not alone, and that they should try to breathe slowly and normally.

steps.instruction: Note the exact time symptoms started and what the symptoms are — tell the paramedics this immediately when they arrive.
steps.detail: Symptom onset time determines which treatments are available. Some cardiac interventions have tight time windows.

steps.instruction: Monitor consciousness and breathing. If the person becomes unconscious and stops breathing normally, begin CPR immediately.
steps.detail: Cardiac arrest — when the heart stops — can follow a heart attack. Be ready to begin CPR without delay.
steps.warning: Cardiac arrest and heart attack are different. Heart attack: person is conscious, in pain. Cardiac arrest: unconscious, not breathing. If you're not sure, start CPR.

steps.instruction: Call 911 immediately.
steps.detail: Stroke treatment is extremely time-sensitive. Clot-busting medication and interventions are only effective within a narrow time window. Every minute counts.

steps.instruction: Note the exact time symptoms first appeared. This is the most important piece of information for the treating team.
steps.detail: Stroke treatment decisions depend entirely on how long ago symptoms started. Use the button below to record the current time as symptom onset.
steps.warning: If the person woke up with symptoms, the onset time is when they were last seen normal — record that time instead.

steps.instruction: Perform the FAST assessment: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call 911.
steps.detail: Ask them to smile — is one side drooping? Raise both arms — does one drift down? Say a simple sentence — is speech slurred or strange? Any of these means stroke.

steps.instruction: Help the person lie down with their head and shoulders slightly elevated — about 30 degrees. Keep them still.
steps.detail: This position reduces strain on the brain. If the person is confused, speak calmly and slowly. Reassure them that help is on the way.

steps.instruction: Do not give the person food, water, or any medications.
steps.detail: Stroke can affect swallowing and cause choking. Medication that thins the blood may be harmful depending on the type of stroke.
steps.warning: Even if the person asks for water or medication, do not give anything by mouth until paramedics or doctors assess them.

steps.instruction: Do not let the person sleep. Keep them awake and talking to you.
steps.detail: Loss of consciousness during a stroke is a sign of deterioration. Monitor and talk to them continuously.

steps.instruction: If the person is at home, unlock the front door so paramedics can enter quickly.
steps.detail: Paramedics may arrive when you are occupied with the patient. An unlocked door prevents delay.

steps.instruction: If the person becomes unconscious and stops breathing normally, begin CPR immediately.
steps.warning: Stroke can progress to cardiac arrest. If they stop breathing, CPR is the right response.

steps.instruction: Stay calm and stay with the person. Do not try to restrain them — let the seizure happen.
steps.detail: Restraining a person during a seizure can cause injury to them or to you. The body's movements during a seizure cannot be stopped.
steps.warning: Never hold the person down, never put anything in their mouth. The idea that people can swallow their tongue is a myth.

steps.instruction: Clear the area: move furniture, hard objects, and anything sharp or dangerous away from the person.
steps.detail: Focus on creating a safe empty space around them. Move a coffee table, push away chairs — anything within arm or leg reach.

steps.instruction: Protect the head: place something soft underneath it — a folded jacket, a pillow, your hands if nothing else is available.
steps.detail: The head is most vulnerable to injury during a seizure. Head protection is the most important physical action you can take.

steps.instruction: Note the time the seizure started. Use the timer below to track how long it lasts.
steps.detail: Seizure duration is medically critical. A seizure lasting more than 5 minutes is a medical emergency requiring 911.
steps.warning: If the timer reaches 5 minutes and the seizure has not stopped, call 911 immediately if you have not already.

steps.instruction: Call 911 if: the seizure lasts more than 5 minutes, a second seizure begins, the person is not breathing after the seizure, there is an injury, or this is their first known seizure.
steps.detail: Also call 911 if the person is pregnant, diabetic, or in water. Any of these conditions makes 911 mandatory regardless of seizure length.

steps.instruction: When the convulsions stop, gently roll the person onto their side into the recovery position.
steps.detail: This prevents saliva or vomit from blocking the airway. Support their head and keep the airway clear.

steps.instruction: Stay with the person until they are fully conscious and aware of their surroundings. This recovery period can take 10 to 30 minutes.
steps.detail: After a seizure, people are often confused, exhausted, and disoriented — this is called the postictal state. Speak calmly and reassure them.
steps.warning: If the person does not regain consciousness or does not return to their normal state within 30 minutes, call 911.

steps.instruction: If the person stops breathing after the seizure, begin CPR immediately.
steps.warning: Respiratory arrest after a seizure is rare but possible. If breathing stops, do not wait — begin CPR.

steps.instruction: Check for danger before approaching: look for traffic, fire, electrical hazards, or anything that could harm you.
steps.detail: You cannot help someone else if you become injured yourself. A safe responder is always more valuable than a brave one.

steps.instruction: Check for response: tap the person's shoulders and shout 'Are you okay?'
steps.detail: A responsive person can tell you what happened and guide your help. An unresponsive person may need CPR.

steps.instruction: Call 911 if you believe this is a serious or life-threatening emergency. When in doubt, call.
steps.detail: Dispatchers are trained to help you determine the right response. Calling 911 and being told you do not need it is far better than not calling when you do.

steps.instruction: Keep the person still and calm. Do not move them unless they are in immediate danger from the environment.
steps.detail: Moving an injured person, especially one with a possible neck or back injury, can worsen the injury. Keep them where they are unless the environment is actively dangerous.
steps.warning: If you must move the person — for example, from a fire or traffic — support the head and neck and move them as a single unit.

steps.instruction: Monitor breathing and consciousness continuously until help arrives. Tell paramedics what happened, when symptoms started, and any changes you observed.
steps.detail: Your observations between the time of injury or collapse and the arrival of paramedics are medically important. Note everything you can.
